summary: Candler Park's beloved retail strip is bouncing back with new boutiques and a bookstore filling vacant storefronts. The Hawks face elimination tonight in a must-win Game 6, while Morris Brown celebrates its largest graduating class in decades after regaining accreditation. Gas prices surge past $3.75 as global tensions persist.

topStory.headline: Candler Park's Charming Retail Strip Gets New Life With Boutiques and Bookstore
topStory.summary: After losing longtime tenants like Flying Biscuit and Dr. Bombay's, the historic McLendon Avenue commercial strip is staging a retail comeback with a writing studio and luxury gift shop.
topStory.body:
One of eastside Atlanta's most cherished neighborhood retail hubs is quickly changing, and this time for the better.

Following the closure of longtime tenants Flying Biscuit Cafe, Kelly's Closet, and Dr. Bombay's Underwater Tea Party in recent years, the historic commercial strip in the 1600 block of McLendon Avenue is undergoing a retail resurgence.

In part of Flying Biscuit's former space (the original cafe location, opened in 1993), Atlanta-based writing studio and nonprofit Lostintheletters opened a brick-and-mortar home base last year. It now serves as a bookstore and creative space.

Immediately next door, a boutique concept called Lucille's is set to open in coming weeks. Described as a 'luxury gift store for the romantically inclined,' expect a curated selection of home decor, candles, books, jewelry, and accessories with a focus on sustainability, wellness, and pleasure.

The new tenants join established favorites Candler Park Market and Gigi's Italian Kitchen on the opposite side of the street, signaling renewed confidence in one of Atlanta's most walkable neighborhood retail corridors.
topStory.imageAlt: McLendon Avenue retail strip in Candler Park

stories.headline: City Council Seeks Transparency on $80M Housing Trust Fund Spending
stories.summary: Since 2021, the Affordable Housing Trust Fund has funneled over $80 million to various initiatives with little public visibility. New legislation aims to change that.
stories.body:
Since its 2021 creation, Atlanta's Affordable Housing Trust Fund has funneled over $80 million to initiatives ranging from affordable housing production to city employee salaries to interest payments on housing bonds, often with little public transparency.

City Councilmember Matt Westmoreland, who authored the original legislation creating the fund, has introduced a resolution urging Mayor Andre Dickens' administration to produce annual reports detailing how much affordable housing has been created versus how much has been spent on auxiliary programs.

The council's Community Development and Human Services Committee unanimously approved the resolution on April 28, teeing it up for a full council vote next week.

'There's a universal desire to have a better and more publicly digestible explanation for how these dollars are getting used every year,' Westmoreland said. The Affordable Housing Trust Fund will receive an estimated $20 million for fiscal year 2027.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta City Council meeting on housing

stories.headline: New Wellness Destination Opens in Buckhead and Midtown With Hybrid Medical-Spa Model
stories.summary: LumiVita blends medical care with spa-style treatments, emphasizing science-backed approaches delivered in a calming setting.
stories.body:
LumiVita has opened new locations in Buckhead and Midtown, bringing a hybrid wellness concept that combines medical care with spa-style services.

The clinics emphasize science-backed treatments delivered in a calming, spa-like setting, designed for ongoing care rather than one-time visits. Medical professionals guide the long-term health and well-being approach.

'We set out to create something Atlanta didn't yet have: a wellness destination that feels as restorative and beautiful as it is medically sophisticated,' says CEO Deirdre Kiely. 'Too often, people feel they must choose between clinical care and a calming wellness experience. LumiVita bridges that gap.'

The brand hosts a grand opening event on April 30 from 6 to 8 p.m., featuring a sensory journey through the space and services. Membership programs are available.

stories.headline: Bar Ana Brings Luxe Desserts and Cocktails to Poncey-Highland
stories.summary: James Beard semifinalist pastry chef Claudia Martinez finally has a place to call her own, a lush hideaway showcasing creative seasonal desserts.
stories.body:
Atlanta's acclaimed pastry chef Claudia Martinez finally has a place to call her own. The multitime James Beard semifinalist cut the ribbon late last year for Bar Ana, a lush little spot showcasing her creative, seasonal desserts.

Finding your way there takes a minute. The bar is in the basement of El Ponce Mexican restaurant in Poncey-Highland. Visitors access it via a serpentine route through the parking lot, across the back patio, and around the building's corner to a dark-green door marked Bar Ana in stylish gold font.

Past the cave-like vestibule, you'll find a chic retreat awash in warm shades of green, rose, gold, and ivory. The design features dark wood floors, stucco arches, and a dramatic ceiling trellis laced with vines.

Standout drinks include the Ana daiquiri with Caribbean rum, passion fruit, vanilla, and cardamom, and the Guava G&T. Mayim Williams, an alum of Kimball House, leads the beverage program. Martinez takes inspiration from her Southern roots and Latin heritage for the dessert menu.

stories.headline: Eater Atlanta Updates Its Essential 38 Restaurants List for Spring
stories.summary: The quarterly guide adds Sammy's sandwich shop in Adair Park, longtime institution Alon's Bakery, and neighborhood wine bar Madeira Park to the essential dining list.
stories.body:
The Eater 38 is the ultimate guide to restaurants that define Atlanta's dining scene. Eater's editors, who are constantly eating out and checking in with restaurant industry insiders, compile this list every quarter.

New to the April 2026 update: local favorite sandwich and coffee shop Sammy's in Adair Park, longtime institution Alon's Bakery, and convivial neighborhood wine bar Madeira Park join the list.

The update also notes significant leadership changes at longtime Eater 38 mainstay and one-Michelin-starred Lazy Betty, including the departures of co-founder and executive chef Aaron Phillips, chef de cuisine Austin Goetzman, beverage director Conrad Helms, and general manager Shannon Dunlap. Eater will continue tracking the restaurant as it evolves.

As always, the list includes a diverse range of cuisines, prices, and neighborhoods across Atlanta. Restaurants are listed in geographical order, not by merit ranking.
stories.imageAlt: Lemon pepper frog legs at Avize restaurant

stories.headline: ATDC Launches North Star AI Labs to Help Georgia Founders Build With AI
stories.summary: Georgia Tech's startup incubator opens a physical lab with server infrastructure and coworking space for entrepreneurs testing AI ideas.
stories.body:
For 45 years, ATDC has helped Georgia founders build. Now it's adding something new: North Star AI Labs, designed to be a launching pad for Georgia founders looking to build with AI.

The space is part physical, part digital. The physical component functions like a mini data center, with a server hub where entrepreneurs can test and iterate on ideas without needing a massive budget or enterprise tech contracts.

Alongside the lab is an AI Lounge and coworking space. On any given day you'll find students working on projects or community members attending workshops and networking. The Georgia Tech AI Club is already involved, and more student organizations are in the pipeline.

James Harris, ATDC's entrepreneur-in-residence and the driving force behind the lab, says it's designed to help local founders access 'help, hardware, and talent.' The equipment, sourced from Georgia Tech surplus, gives the space a scrappy, true startup feel.
stories.imageAlt: ATDC North Star AI Labs space

stories.headline: Hawks Face Elimination Tonight in Must-Win Game 6 Against Knicks
stories.summary: After back-to-back blowout losses, Atlanta returns to State Farm Arena needing a win to force a deciding Game 7 in New York.
stories.body:
The Atlanta Hawks know there are only two options tonight: win and force a Game 7, or lose and go home.

After winning Game 2 and Game 3 by a combined two points, the Hawks have been blown out in Game 4 and Game 5. Knicks guard Jalen Brunson scored 39 points to lead New York to a 29-point win on Tuesday night, giving the Knicks a 3-2 series lead.

'We still got a chance. It's 3-2. We're going home to protect our home court,' Atlanta guard Dyson Daniels said. 'I mean, we got to be better.'

For the Hawks, Jalen Johnson led the team in Game 5 with 18 points, 10 rebounds and six assists, while Onyeka Okongwu added 16 points and Daniels contributed 17. But Atlanta has struggled to contain New York's offensive production in the last two matchups.

Game 6 tips off at State Farm Arena at 7 p.m. All fans in attendance will receive a Hawks rally towel.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta Hawks playoff basketball at State Farm Arena

stories.headline: Morris Brown Celebrates Largest Graduating Class in Decades After Regaining Accreditation
stories.summary: The Atlanta HBCU has earned back its accreditation after losing it over claims of financial mismanagement, marking a major turnaround.
stories.body:
Heading into their 2026 graduation ceremony, students and administrators at Morris Brown College have something special to celebrate.

The Atlanta HBCU has earned back its accreditation after losing it over claims of financial mismanagement. The accreditation was granted by the Transnational Association of Christian Colleges and Schools, an agency in Virginia.

'Morris Brown's reaffirmation of accreditation through 2031, earned during our 145th anniversary year, makes this even more special,' President Kevin James said. 'It affirms the institution's adherence to quality and reflects continuous improvement over the past five years since regaining our accreditation following a nearly 20-year hiatus.'

The Southern Association of Colleges and Schools revoked Morris Brown's accreditation in 2002 after a former president and the college's former financial aid director inflated enrollment data to steal federal funds. Both later pleaded guilty to embezzling federal funds.

James' tenure has been marked by fundraising highs for the school as it rebuilds its reputation and enrollment.

stories.headline: Supreme Court Voting Rights Decision Sparks Immediate Georgia Redistricting Debate
stories.summary: Hours after the court struck down a Louisiana electoral map, some Georgia Republicans called for redrawing congressional districts before the midterms.
stories.body:
A U.S. Supreme Court decision striking down a Louisiana electoral map is already fueling political debate in Georgia, with some Republicans pushing to redraw congressional districts ahead of the midterm elections.

The justices voted 6-3 to reject a Louisiana map that would have created a second majority-Black district, ruling it relied too heavily on race and effectively limiting the use of racial considerations in drawing electoral boundaries.

In Georgia, state Sen. Greg Dolezal is calling on Gov. Brian Kemp to convene a special legislative session to redraw congressional maps. 'This is a time to be bold. This is a time to be aggressive,' Dolezal said.

Governor Kemp's office said he is 'analyzing this ruling and its potential impacts.' Georgia lawmakers were ordered to redraw the state's maps during a special session in 2023 after a federal judge ruled that the state's congressional and legislative boundary lines diluted the voting strength of Black Georgians.

U.S. Sen. Raphael Warnock criticized the decision, warning it could lead to 'rigged maps.'

stories.headline: Emory University Expels Law Student Who Sent Racist Emails to Faculty
stories.summary: The university has been quietly dealing with a law student who sent increasingly bizarre messages containing racist language.
stories.body:
Emory University has expelled a law student who sent increasingly bizarre messages containing racist language to a faculty member.

The university confirmed the student's expulsion after reports surfaced about the disturbing communications. Details about the specific content of the messages have not been publicly released, but university officials characterized them as containing racist language that escalated over time.

Emory Law School has approximately 900 students and is consistently ranked among the top law schools in the Southeast. The incident highlights ongoing challenges universities face in addressing hate speech and misconduct within their student bodies.

The university has not released the student's name, citing privacy concerns related to student disciplinary matters.

stories.headline: Gas Prices Surge Past $3.75 in Georgia as Global Tensions Persist
stories.summary: Prices jumped five cents overnight and are up more than 80 cents compared to last year, with some metro counties approaching $3.90 per gallon.
stories.body:
Gas prices are climbing again across Georgia, adding more pressure to drivers already dealing with higher costs at the pump.

The statewide average is hovering around $3.74 to $3.75 per gallon, marking a sharp increase of more than 19 cents in just one week and more than 80 cents compared to this time last year. Prices increased five cents overnight.

Prices vary widely by county. Oconee County leads at $3.89, followed by Forsyth at $3.84 and Fulton at $3.83. Even with the increase, Georgia prices remain below the national average, which is now above $4.20 per gallon.

Analysts point to ongoing global instability, particularly tensions involving Iran and disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz, as a major driver behind rising fuel costs. That region is a key artery for global oil supply, and any slowdown in shipments quickly ripples into higher prices at the pump.

Consumer adviser Clark Howard predicts the most expensive stations in metro Atlanta are 'most likely going to hit $5 a gallon in the next couple of weeks.'

stories.headline: Braves Stay Unbeaten in Series Play After Olson's Walk-Off Homer Against Tigers
stories.summary: Matt Olson's two-run shot in the ninth inning off closer Kenley Jansen lifted Atlanta to a 22-9 record, the best in baseball.
stories.body:
The Atlanta Braves still haven't lost a series this season. They are 22-9 and the best team in baseball after getting a game-winning two-run home run from Matt Olson in the ninth inning.

'I didn't think it was going out,' said Olson after the game. 'That thing kept floating for me.'

Olson hit the game-winner off Detroit Tigers closer Kenley Jansen, a future Hall of Fame player. Ozzie Albies had walked to begin the ninth inning, and a few minutes later, Olson was meeting the rest of the Braves at home plate as fans cheered.

The walk-off home run was Olson's first as a Brave and his fourth overall. He hit three game-winning home runs for the Oakland A's before moving to Atlanta.

'That's a good one right there. We've had a few of those already, so that's a good sign,' said first-year Braves manager Walt Weiss of another late-inning celebration.

It was also Chris Sale bobblehead night at Truist Park.
stories.imageAlt: Matt Olson celebrates walk-off home run
